GCD of exponentials with CRT-composite modulus and lcm-order forcing

Let $M=17^3\cdot 11^2$.

Let $a$ be the least positive integer satisfying
$a\equiv 18 \pmod{17^3}$ and $a\equiv 12 \pmod{11^2}$.

For each integer $t\ge 1$, define
$G_t=\gcd\!\left(a^{37t+30}-1,\ a^{26t+107}-1\right)$.

1) Prove that the set $\{t\ge 1:\ M\mid G_t\}$ is nonempty and that for all $t\ge 1$,
$$
M\mid G_t
\iff \mathrm{ord}_{M}(a)\mid \gcd(37t+30,\ 26t+107)
\iff 3179\mid(37t+30).
$$

2) Let $t_0$ be the least positive integer $t$ such that $M\mid G_t$.
Since the natural output is the integer $t_0$, define the reported answer to be the unique integer $N$ with $0\le N<1000$ and $N\equiv t_0\pmod{1000}$.
Compute $N$.

Solution.
Step 1 (Euclidean_algorithm_on_exponents): For integers $X,Y$ and any integer $q$, we have $\gcd(X,Y)=\gcd(X,Y-qX)$, because an integer divides both $X$ and $Y$ iff it divides both $X$ and $Y-qX$.

Step 2 (Euclidean_algorithm_on_exponents): For $A>1$ and $u,v\in\mathbb N$,
$$
\gcd(A^u-1,\ A^v-1)=A^{\gcd(u,v)}-1.
$$
Proof sketch: write $v=qu+r$ with $0\le r<u$; then $A^v-1=(A^u-1)\cdot(\text{integer})+(A^r-1)$, so $\gcd(A^u-1,A^v-1)=\gcd(A^u-1,A^r-1)$ by Step 1 (from Step 1). Iterating Euclid on the pair $(u,v)$ ends at $\gcd(u,v)$, giving the identity.

Step 3 (order_arguments_mod_prime_power): If $\gcd(A,M)=1$ and $d:=\mathrm{ord}_M(A)$ is the least positive integer with $A^d\equiv 1\pmod M$, then
$$
M\mid(A^t-1) \iff d\mid t.
$$
Indeed, if $t=qd+r$ with $0\le r<d$, then $A^t\equiv (A^d)^qA^r\equiv A^r\pmod M$, so $M\mid(A^t-1)$ forces $M\mid(A^r-1)$ and hence $r=0$ by minimality of $d$; the reverse direction is immediate.

Step 4 (Chinese_remainder_theorem): Since $\gcd(17^3,11^2)=1$, the Chinese remainder theorem gives a unique residue class modulo $M=17^3\cdot 11^2$ satisfying the two congruences for $a$, hence a unique least positive representative. In particular, $a\equiv 18\equiv 1\pmod{17}$ and $a\equiv 12\equiv 1\pmod{11}$, so $\gcd(a,M)=1$.

Step 5 (order_arguments_mod_prime_power): (valuation-based binomial lemma) Let $p$ be an odd prime and $k\ge 2$. Then
$$
\mathrm{ord}_{p^k}(1+p)=p^{k-1}.
$$
Proof: set $n=p^{k-1}$. By the binomial theorem,
$(1+p)^n-1=\binom{n}{1}p+\sum_{j\ge 2}\binom{n}{j}p^j=p^k+\sum_{j\ge 2}\binom{n}{j}p^j$.
For $j\ge 2$, use $\binom{n}{j}=\frac{n}{j}\binom{n-1}{j-1}$ to get
$v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{j}\right)\ge v_p(n)-v_p(j)=(k-1)-v_p(j)$, hence
$v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{j}p^j\right)\ge (k-1)-v_p(j)+j=(k-1)+(j-v_p(j))\ge k$,
since $j-v_p(j)\ge 1$ for every $j\ge 2$. Therefore $(1+p)^{p^{k-1}}\equiv 1\pmod{p^k}$.

For minimality, set $n'=p^{k-2}$ (so $n'=1$ if $k=2$). Then
$(1+p)^{n'}-1=p^{k-1}+\sum_{j\ge 2}\binom{n'}{j}p^j$.
For $j\ge 2$, the same estimate gives
$v_p\!\left(\binom{n'}{j}p^j\right)\ge (k-2)-v_p(j)+j=(k-2)+(j-v_p(j))$.
For odd $p$ and $j\ge 2$ we have $j-v_p(j)\ge 2$: if $p\nmid j$ then $v_p(j)=0$ and $j\ge 2$; if $p\mid j$ then $v_p(j)=e\ge 1$ and $j\ge p^e\ge 3^e\ge e+2$, so $j-e\ge 2$. Hence every $j\ge 2$ term is divisible by $p^k$, giving
$(1+p)^{p^{k-2}}\equiv 1+p^{k-1}\not\equiv 1\pmod{p^k}$.

So the order divides $p^{k-1}$ (because $(1+p)^{p^{k-1}}\equiv 1$) but does not divide $p^{k-2}$, forcing $\mathrm{ord}_{p^k}(1+p)=p^{k-1}$.

Step 6 (order_arguments_mod_prime_power): From Step 4, we have $a\equiv 1+17\pmod{17^3}$ and $a\equiv 1+11\pmod{11^2}$ (from Step 4). Applying Step 5 with $(p,k)=(17,3)$ and $(11,2)$ (from Step 5) gives
$$
\mathrm{ord}_{17^3}(a)=17^2=289,\qquad \mathrm{ord}_{11^2}(a)=11.
$$

Step 7 (CRT_order_composition): Since $\gcd(17^3,11^2)=1$, CRT gives
$(\mathbb Z/M\mathbb Z)^\times\cong (\mathbb Z/17^3\mathbb Z)^\times\times(\mathbb Z/11^2\mathbb Z)^\times$.
The order in a direct product is the lcm of component orders, so
$$
\mathrm{ord}_M(a)=\mathrm{lcm}(\mathrm{ord}_{17^3}(a),\ \mathrm{ord}_{11^2}(a))
=\mathrm{lcm}(289,11)=3179,
$$
using Step 6 (from Step 6).

Step 8 (Euclidean_algorithm_on_exponents): Let $E_1=37t+30$ and $E_2=26t+107$, and set $g_t=\gcd(E_1,E_2)$. Then
$$
G_t=\gcd(a^{E_1}-1,\ a^{E_2}-1)=a^{g_t}-1
$$
by Step 2 (from Step 2).

Step 9 (order_to_Bezout_bridge): By Step 8 (from Step 8), $M\mid G_t$ is equivalent to $a^{g_t}\equiv 1\pmod M$. Since $\gcd(a,M)=1$ from Step 4 (from Step 4), Step 3 applies (from Step 3) and yields
$$
M\mid G_t \iff \mathrm{ord}_M(a)\mid g_t.
$$

Step 10 (Bezout_on_linear_exponents): Any common divisor of $E_1$ and $E_2$ divides any integer linear combination. Compute
$$
37E_2-26E_1=37(26t+107)-26(37t+30)=37\cdot 107-26\cdot 30=3179.
$$
Hence $g_t=\gcd(E_1,E_2)$ divides $3179$ for every $t\ge 1$.

Step 11 (Bezout_on_linear_exponents): Let $d:=\gcd(E_1,3179)$. Then $d\mid E_1$ and $d\mid 3179=37E_2-26E_1$, so $d\mid 37E_2$. Because $\gcd(37,3179)=1$, we conclude $d\mid E_2$, so $d$ is a common divisor of $E_1,E_2$ and thus $d\le g_t$. Conversely, Step 10 gives $g_t\mid 3179$ (from Step 10), so $g_t\le \gcd(E_1,3179)=d$. Therefore
$$
g_t=\gcd(E_1,E_2)=\gcd(E_1,3179).
$$

Step 12 (order_to_Bezout_bridge): Insert $\mathrm{ord}_M(a)=3179$ from Step 7 (from Step 7) into Step 9 (from Step 9) to get $M\mid G_t\iff 3179\mid g_t$. Using Step 11 (from Step 11), this is
$$
M\mid G_t\iff 3179\mid \gcd(E_1,3179)\iff 3179\mid E_1\iff 3179\mid (37t+30).
$$
Since $\gcd(37,3179)=1$, the congruence $37t\equiv -30\pmod{3179}$ has solutions, so the solution set is nonempty.

Step 13 (extended_Euclidean_algorithm): Solve $37t\equiv -30\pmod{3179}$ from Step 12 (from Step 12) by finding $37^{-1}\pmod{3179}$ via Euclid:
$3179=37\cdot 85+34$,
$37=34\cdot 1+3$,
$34=3\cdot 11+1$.
Back-substitution gives $1=2148\cdot 37-25\cdot 3179$, so $37^{-1}\equiv 2148\pmod{3179}$.
Thus
$t\equiv (-30)\cdot 2148\equiv -64440\equiv 2319\pmod{3179}$.

Step 14 (order_to_Bezout_bridge): Because $\gcd(37,3179)=1$, Step 12 implies the congruence class for $t$ is unique modulo $3179$ (from Step 12). With the class found in Step 13 (from Step 13), the least positive solution is
$$
t_0=2319.
$$

Step 15 (order_to_Bezout_bridge): The normalization requests $N\equiv t_0\pmod{1000}$ with $0\le N<1000$. Using $t_0=2319$ from Step 14 (from Step 14), we get $N\equiv 2319\equiv 319\pmod{1000}$, hence $N=319$.
Answer: 319

Uniqueness Test
From Step 12, $M\mid G_t$ holds exactly when $37t+30\equiv 0\pmod{3179}$.

Since $\gcd(37,3179)=1$, this linear congruence has exactly one residue class modulo $3179$. Step 13 computes that class as $t\equiv 2319\pmod{3179}$, so there is a unique least positive solution $t_0=2319$. Therefore the normalized output is uniquely forced to be $t_0\bmod 1000=2319\bmod 1000=319$.

319
